project.title: Proteomics of extracellular vesicles from Fusarium oxysporum
project.projectDescription: Through proteomic analysis using mass spectrometry, a total of 1,931 proteins enriched in Fo-EVs were identified. Among these proteins, 350 were found to contain signal peptides and 375 possessed transmembrane domains. GO annotation and enrichment analysis, using the whole-genome proteins as background, revealed that these Fo-EVs proteins were primarily involved in cell wall synthesis, vesicle transport, and pathogenicity-related metabolic pathways. Furthermore, nine potential fungal EVs markers were screened, including Hsp70, Rho GTPase family proteins, and SNARE proteins. This study established a comprehensive Fo-EVs protein library and marker database, providing valuable proteomic resources for understanding the biological functions of fungal extracellular vesicles.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: The sample processing protocol for Fo-EVs proteomic analysis begins with solid-state fungal culture and UC-SEC purification, followed by protein extraction using RIPA lysis buffer and concentration with 3 kDa ultrafiltration tubes. After quantifying proteins by BCA assay and separating via SDS-PAGE with silver staining, mass spectrometry analysis is performed using Data-Independent Acquisition on an Astral mass spectrometer. Proteins are screened based on abundance consistency and peptide uniqueness, then functionally annotated through Transmembrane Domain and Signal Peptide identification via Uniprot, GO annotation using eggNOG, enzyme classification by EC numbers, and GO enrichment analysis with g:Profiler against the whole-genome background.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Mass spectrometry data processing was performed using a standardized computational pipeline. Raw data acquired through Data-Independent Acquisition (DIA) on the Astral mass spectrometer were processed with DIA-NN software for database searching and quantitative analysis. Quality control metrics were rigorously monitored, including peptide mass error (measured in ppm), trypsin-digested peptide length distribution, and Pearson Correlation Coefficients (PCC) among biological replicates. Protein identification followed strict filtering criteria, requiring a maximum abundance change ratio ≤1.2 between replicate experiments and the presence of at least two unique peptide segments per protein. For functional annotation, screened proteins were analyzed through Uniprot to identify transmembrane domains (TMD) and signal peptides (SP), followed by Gene Ontology (GO) annotation using eggNOG for both TMD-containing and non-TMD proteins. Enzyme classification was performed based on Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers, and GO enrichment analysis was conducted using g:Profiler with the entire genome protein complement as background, applying stringent statistical thresholds (P < 0.001, FDR < 0.05) to identify significantly enriched biological processes and pathways.Mass spectrometry data processing was performed using a standardized computational pipeline. Raw data acquired through Data-Independent Acquisition (DIA) on the Astral mass spectrometer were processed with DIA-NN software for database searching and quantitative analysis. Quality control metrics were rigorously monitored, including peptide mass error (measured in ppm), trypsin-digested peptide length distribution, and Pearson Correlation Coefficients (PCC) among biological replicates. Protein identification followed strict filtering criteria, requiring a maximum abundance change ratio ≤1.2 between replicate experiments and the presence of at least two unique peptide segments per protein. For functional annotation, screened proteins were analyzed through Uniprot to identify transmembrane domains (TMD) and signal peptides (SP), followed by Gene Ontology (GO) annotation using eggNOG for both TMD-containing and non-TMD proteins. Enzyme classification was performed based on Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers, and GO enrichment analysis was conducted using g:Profiler with the entire genome protein complement as background, applying stringent statistical thresholds (P < 0.001, FDR < 0.05) to identify significantly enriched biological processes and pathways.

references.referenceLine: Lou J, Hu G, Wang X, Liu Q, Chen Y, Zhao W. Establishment of an Isolation System for Extracellular Vesicles of <i>Fusarium oxysporum</i> and Its Proteomic Analysis. J Fungi (Basel). 2025 11(12):884

Characteristics[NumberOfTechnicalReplica: a total of 5 technical replicates were performed.
Characteristics[SpikedCompound]: 5 μL of Dio fluorescent probe (Lanjieke Technology, Beijing, China)
Comment[Instrument]: Vanquish Neo Nano LC System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Shanghai, China)
Astral High-Resolution Mass Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Shanghai, China)
Comment[FlowRateChromatogram]: The flow rate was set at 2 μL/min.
Comment[CollisionEnergy]: The collision energy was set at 25 eV (higher-energy collisional dissociation, HCD)
